response: The global food system faces unprecedented pressure as the world population is projected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, requiring about a 60% increase in food production. Climate change is lowering yields through droughts, floods, and extreme temperatures, while agriculture already accounts for roughly 25% of greenhouse gas emissions and water scarcity threatens major farming regions (including rapid depletion of the Ogallala Aquifer). Soil degradation has reduced productivity on 33% of the world’s arable land, and about one-third of produced food is lost or wasted between farm and fork. Proposed responses include precision agriculture, vertical farming, alternative proteins, drought-resistant genetic modification, and regenerative farming, along with policy reforms—though the key challenge is scaling solutions affordably for the world’s poorest populations.
